Q1: I tried to keep a consistent study routine, usually dedicating about 25 hours across the week. I would block out specific times in the morning and evening when I knew I could focus without interruptions. Being a kinesthetic learner, I needed to actually do things to understand them, so I would often work through practice problems or create physical notes while studying. My routine was pretty steady, even if my attendance in live sessions wasn't always perfect.
Q2: I generally stuck to regular times each week because that structure helped me stay on track. Mondays and Wednesdays were my heavy study days, while weekends I would catch up on anything I missed. Sometimes deadlines would push me to adjust, but honestly, I preferred having that predictable rhythm. It just felt easier to maintain motivation when I knew exactly when I was supposed to be studying.
Q3: Honestly, I logged in mainly when I needed to check for new assignments or see if there were announcements I had missed. Sometimes I would log in just to download materials that I could work with offline. It wasn't something I did daily, maybe a couple of times a week. I knew what I needed to get done, so I would check in, grab what I needed, and then do the actual work elsewhere.
Q4: Using the LMS did become somewhat routine for me, especially at the start of each week when new content was posted. I would log in on Monday mornings to see what was coming up and plan my week accordingly. It wasn't like I was constantly on there, but it became a regular checkpoint. The peer support group I was part of actually helped remind me to check in regularly too, which was surprisingly helpful.
Q5: The video lectures were really useful to me because I could watch them and then physically practice what was being taught. I watched quite a lot of video content, probably close to three hours worth. The discussion forums were also helpful when I needed clarification on something confusing. I appreciated being able to see how other students approached problems because it gave me different perspectives to try out.
Q6: I'll admit I didn't always complete all the readings or optional supplementary materials. Sometimes they felt repetitive or too theoretical for my learning style. I needed hands-on practice more than reading lengthy documents. If something didn't seem immediately applicable to assignments or exams, I would push it aside and focus on what I knew would be assessed.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to what I needed to know for assignments or exams. If I could practice something and feel myself getting better at it, that was valuable to me. I also appreciated activities that had clear instructions and expected outcomes. Anything too vague or open-ended made me question whether my time was being well spent.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different because there was no pressure attached to them. I would look at them and think about whether I had time after finishing the required work. Sometimes I engaged with optional content if it looked interesting or if my peer support group mentioned it was helpful. But honestly, required activities always came first because those were what counted toward my grade.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying offline that the LMS would never capture. I would download lecture slides and videos, then work through them on my own with handwritten notes. I also used physical flashcards and practiced problems in a notebook. Being a kinesthetic learner, I needed to write things out and move around while studying, which obviously doesn't show up in any online system.
Q10: There were many times when I was deeply engaged with course material but completely offline. I would print out problem sets and work through them at my desk or even while walking around my room explaining concepts out loud. Sometimes I studied with peers in person, discussing difficult topics for hours. None of that productive study time would appear in my LMS activity logs at all.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my preferred method. I would access a video or document once, download it, and then work with it repeatedly offline over several days. I might watch a lecture video three or four times while taking notes, but the LMS would only show one view. This was especially true for exam preparation when I was reviewing materials I had downloaded weeks earlier.
Q12: There were moments when life got busy and engagement became harder. Sometimes family responsibilities or just general fatigue made it difficult to stay focused. Around midterms, I felt a bit overwhelmed with the number of courses I was taking. But honestly, my stress levels stayed pretty manageable throughout, and I found ways to push through those difficult periods.
Q13: Having 14 online courses was definitely a lot to juggle, and sometimes I had to prioritize which courses got my attention on any given day. Fatigue would sometimes make me less likely to log into the LMS and more likely to just work with materials I already had downloaded. When I was tired, the thought of navigating the platform felt like extra work, so I would just study offline instead.
Q14: There were definitely times when I logged in and scrolled through content without really absorbing anything. Sometimes I would open a video and let it play while my mind wandered elsewhere. I think this happened most when I was tired or when the material didn't connect with anything practical. Those moments felt unproductive even though technically I was 'active' on the platform.
Q15: I did postpone some assignments, which is why my completion rate wasn't as high as I would have liked. Sometimes I avoided tasks that seemed confusing or that I didn't feel prepared for. I knew they were important, but I would tell myself I would do them after I understood the material better. Looking back, I should have asked for help sooner instead of avoiding those challenging tasks.
Q16: Deadlines definitely influenced when I would log into the LMS and how intensely I engaged with materials. I would check the calendar and work backward to plan my study time. Close to deadlines, my LMS activity would spike as I submitted work and double-checked requirements. Without deadlines, I might have been even less active on the platform because my offline studying felt more productive.
Q17: Feedback did affect my engagement, especially when it was encouraging or showed me where I could improve. Getting good scores on assignments motivated me to keep going with my approach. When feedback was delayed, I sometimes felt uncertain about whether I was on the right track. The peer support intervention was actually really helpful because it gave me informal feedback from classmates too.
Q18: The structure of the course gave me a framework to follow, which I appreciated. Having clear modules and deadlines helped me plan my weekly routine. However, with 14 courses, sometimes the structures overlapped in confusing ways. I did better when course expectations were clearly laid out from the beginning so I could integrate them into my overall schedule.
Q19: The video content being available for download made engagement much easier for me since I could study in my preferred kinesthetic way. Having discussion forums was also helpful for getting peer perspectives. What made things harder was when materials were only accessible online without download options, or when there were too many small tasks that felt like busywork rather than meaningful learning.
Q20: I think students might show high activity because they're exploring, trying to understand what's expected, or searching for resources without necessarily being ready to submit work. They could also be dealing with confusion about what's actually required. Sometimes you need to spend a lot of time understanding something before you can produce anything meaningful. Activity doesn't always equal output.
Q21: That actually reflects my experience quite well. I didn't log in frequently, but I studied extensively offline and performed reasonably well on exams and assignments. My exam score and assignment scores show that I was learning even though my LMS engagement looked low. I think this pattern is common for students who prefer to work independently with downloaded materials rather than constantly being online.
Q22: Course logs can show when someone accessed materials and how long they were on the platform. They can track submission patterns and forum participation. But they completely miss offline study time, the quality of engagement during logged sessions, and learning that happens through physical practice or peer discussions outside the platform. They capture presence but not necessarily learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. For me, it means actively working with concepts until they make sense, asking questions when I'm confused, and connecting new information to what I already know. It's about genuine effort and curiosity, which doesn't always show up in how often you click around on a platform.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show how much time I spent with printed materials, practicing problems by hand, or discussing concepts with my peer support group in person. It wouldn't capture the moments when something finally clicked for me after hours of offline practice. My motivation and genuine interest in learning happened mostly away from the screen, in the physical act of doing and practicing.
